items.q: Where is most digested food absorbed?
items.correct: Most absorption happens in the small intestine, whose villi give a huge surface area for absorbing soluble food molecules.
items.misconception: Food is absorbed mainly in the stomach.
items.random_wrong: Food is absorbed mainly in the large intestine.
items.why: Students associate the stomach with digestion and assume absorption happens there too.

items.correct: Bile emulsifies fats into small droplets, increasing their surface area for lipase, and it neutralises acidic stomach contents.
items.misconception: Bile is an enzyme that digests fats.
items.random_wrong: Bile breaks proteins down into amino acids.
items.why: Students wrongly classify bile as a digestive enzyme rather than an emulsifier.

items.q: Does chewing food count as chemical digestion?
items.correct: No - chewing is physical (mechanical) digestion; chemical digestion is the breakdown of molecules by enzymes.
items.misconception: Chewing is chemical digestion because it breaks food down.
items.random_wrong: Chewing has no role in digestion at all.
items.why: Students conflate physical breakdown with enzyme-driven chemical digestion.

items.q: The enzyme that digests starch is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: amylase. “protease” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Villi increase the rate of absorption mainly because they:
items.correct: increase the surface area of the small intestine
items.why: It's increase the surface area of the small intestine. “produce more digestive enzymes” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The role of bile in digestion is to:
items.why: Correct answer: emulsify fats into small droplets. “digest proteins into amino acids” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A diet lacking vitamin C can cause:
items.why: Correct answer: scurvy. “rickets” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
